stem: Which of the following is a prime number?
solutionStandard: 1 is not prime by definition. \(9=3^2\), \(21=3\times7\), and \(27=3^3\) are all composite. \(23\) has no divisors other than 1 and itself.

stem: \(n\) is an odd integer.
solutionStandard: If \(n=1\): \(n^2=1\), \(n+1=2\), so B is greater. If \(n=3\): \(n^2=9\), \(n+1=4\), so A is greater. Since the relationship changes, it cannot be determined.
solutionShortcut: Plug in two different odd values (e.g. 1 and 3, or a negative odd value like -1) to see the relationship flips — this alone proves the answer is D.

stem: What is the greatest common divisor of 48 and 60?
solutionStandard: \(48 = 2^4 \times 3\) and \(60 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 5\). The GCD takes the lowest power of each shared prime: \(2^2 \times 3 = 12\).

stem: What is the least common multiple of 8 and 12?
solutionStandard: \(8 = 2^3\) and \(12 = 2^2 \times 3\). The LCM takes the highest power of every prime present: \(2^3 \times 3 = 24\).

stem: A number is divisible by 6. Which of the following must also be true? Select all that apply.
solutionStandard: \(6 = 2 \times 3\), so any multiple of 6 is automatically a multiple of 2 and of 3, and every multiple of 2 is even. Divisibility by 12 is not guaranteed (e.g. 18 is divisible by 6 but not 12).

stem: For any integer \(n\), which of the following must be even?
solutionStandard: \(n^2 + n = n(n+1)\), the product of two consecutive integers. One of any two consecutive integers is always even, so the product is always even. (B), (D), and (E) depend on whether \(n\) is even or odd, and (C) is always odd.
solutionShortcut: Plug in one even and one odd value of \(n\) (e.g. \(n=2\) and \(n=3\)) and eliminate any option that isn't even both times.

stem: What is the greatest number of consecutive integers, starting from 1, that can be multiplied together before the product exceeds 200?
solutionStandard: \(1\times2\times3\times4=24\), and \(1\times2\times3\times4\times5=120 \le 200\), but \(\times 6 = 720 > 200\). So 5 integers (through 5!) is the greatest that keeps the product at or below 200.

stem: How many positive divisors does 60 have?
solutionStandard: \(60 = 2^2 \times 3^1 \times 5^1\). The number of divisors is \((2+1)(1+1)(1+1) = 3 \times 2 \times 2 = 12\).

stem: \(p\) and \(q\) are distinct prime numbers, both greater than 2.
solutionStandard: Every prime greater than 2 is odd, so \(p+q\) is always even, but its size relative to 10 is not fixed: \(p=3, q=5\) gives \(p+q=8 < 10\); \(p=3, q=7\) gives \(p+q=10 = 10\); \(p=5, q=7\) gives \(p+q=12 > 10\). Since the relationship changes, it cannot be determined.

stem: \(N\) is the smallest positive integer that is divisible by both 18 and 24. What is the sum of the digits of \(N\)?
What is the greatest common divisor of {{4*k}} and {{6*k}}?
solutionStandard: \(\gcd(4k, 6k) = k \times \gcd(4,6) = 2k\).

stem: Which of the following is NOT a multiple of {{n}}?
solutionStandard: Every option except (D) is \(n\) times a whole number. (D) is \(5n+1\), which leaves a remainder of 1 when divided by \(n\).

stem: What is the least common multiple of {{3*k}} and {{5*k}}?
A number is divisible by 15. Which of the following must also be true? Select all that apply.
solutionStandard: \(15 = 3 \times 5\), so any multiple of 15 is automatically divisible by 3 and by 5. Divisibility by 45 is not guaranteed (e.g. 30 is divisible by 15 but not 45), and 30 is also a counterexample to (D), since it's even.

stem: If \(p\) and \(q\) are distinct prime numbers, how many positive divisors does \(p^2 q\) have?
solutionStandard: The number of divisors of \(p^2 q^1\) is \((2+1)(1+1) = 6\), regardless of which two distinct primes \(p\) and \(q\) are.

stem: What is the greatest number of consecutive integers, starting from 1, whose product does not exceed 30?
solutionStandard: \(1\times2\times3\times4=24 \le 30\), but \(1\times2\times3\times4\times5=120 > 30\). So 4 integers is the greatest that keeps the product at or below 30.

stem: \(n = {{2*k}}\), a positive even integer.
quantityA: The remainder when \(n^2\) is divided by 4
solutionStandard: Since \(n\) is even, \(n=2m\) for some integer \(m\), so \(n^2 = 4m^2\), which is always exactly divisible by 4. The remainder is always 0.

stem: \(N\) is the smallest positive integer that is divisible by both 20 and 30. What is the sum of the digits of \(N\)?
solutionStandard: \(20 = 2^2 \times 5\), \(30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5\). \(\text{lcm} = 2^2 \times 3 \times 5 = 60\). Digit sum of 60 is \(6+0=6\).

stem: If \(x\) is an integer and \(x^3\) is odd, which of the following must be true?
solutionStandard: An odd number cubed is always odd, and an even number cubed is always even. So \(x^3\) odd forces \(x\) to be odd. None of the other properties are required (e.g. \(x=-3\) works: odd, negative, not prime, not a perfect square).

stem: \(x\) is a positive integer such that \(x^2\) has exactly 3 positive divisors.
solutionStandard: A number has exactly 3 positive divisors only if it is the square of a prime (divisors \(1, p, p^2\)). So \(x^2 = p^2\) for some prime \(p\), meaning \(x = p\) itself must be prime — but it could be any prime (2, 3, 5, ...), not necessarily the smallest. If \(x=2\), the quantities are equal; if \(x=3\), Quantity A is greater. The relationship cannot be determined.
